Saul reigned one year, and nothing particular happened; but in his second year the events recorded in this chapter took place. For over a year he gave the Philistines time to prepare for war, and to weaken and disarm the Israelites. When people are confident in themselves, they are often led into foolishness. The chief advantages of the enemies of the church come from the bad behavior of its professed friends. When Saul finally sounded an alarm, the people, dissatisfied with his management or terrified by the power of the enemy, did not come to him, or quickly left him.

Saul broke the order expressly given by Samuel, see ch. , as to what should be done in times of emergency. Saul offered sacrifice without Samuel, and did it himself, though he was neither priest nor prophet. When charged with disobedience, he justified himself in what he had done, and gave no sign of repentance for it. He wanted this act of disobedience to pass as an instance of his prudence, and as proof of his piety. People who lack inner devotion often place great importance on the outward practices of religion. Samuel charges Saul with being an enemy to him. Those that disobey God’s commandments do foolishly for themselves. Sin is folly, and the greatest sinners are the greatest fools. Our willingness to obey or disobey God will often be shown by our behavior in things which seem small. People see only Saul's outward act, which seems minor; but God saw that he did this with unbelief and distrust of his providence, with contempt for his authority and justice, and with rebellion against the light of his own conscience. Blessed Savior, may we never, like Saul, bring our poor offerings, or imagined peace-offerings, without looking to your precious, all-sufficient sacrifice! You only, O Lord, can make, or have made, our peace in the blood of the cross.

See how shrewd the Philistines were when they had power; they not only prevented the people of Israel from making weapons of war, but forced them to depend on their enemies, even for farming tools. How impolitic Saul was, who did not, at the beginning of his reign, try to correct this. A lack of true understanding always accompanies a lack of grace. Sins which seem very small to us have dangerous consequences. It is miserable to be a guilty, defenseless nation; much more those who are without the whole armor of God.
